agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property owners for Trounce Alley & Blood Alley maintenance
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners for the 2025 maintenance costs of Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square. This is a local improvement project where the costs are shared among the properties that benefit from the alleys. If approved, the bylaw would create a special assessment on those properties to cover the work.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property for East Hastings parking project costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a proposed bylaw that would assess (charge a special property fee on) certain real properties to cover the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. If passed, property owners within the defined area would be required to pay this levy to help fund the parking project. The bylaw is still a proposal — no decision has been made yet. Business owners and property owners in the East Hastings area should be aware of this upcoming vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this property levy could affect your land if you own property in the East Hastings area.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the properties at 320–360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract that locks in affordability rules and sets out how these units can be used. If approved, the agreement would be registered on the property title, meaning current and future owners must follow the rules. This proposal has not yet been decided. It could affect developers, landlords, and tenants of these specific buildings.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed amendment to housing agreement at Prior, Malkin, Station — council to vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a proposed bylaw to amend an existing housing agreement for properties at 450–496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. The original agreement (By-law 14507) sets rules for affordable housing at this site. The proposed changes could alter affordability requirements or other terms. This is not yet approved — council will vote at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on amending CD-1 bylaw for 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a proposed bylaw that would amend the CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) zoning rules for the property at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. This is an upcoming decision that could change what is allowed to be built or used on that site.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	A By-law to amend CD-1 (198) By-law No. 6254 (1700-1750 West 75th Avenue)
(Councillor Montague will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning 1710-1730 East Pender Street to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: A proposed bylaw before Burnaby City Council would change the zoning for the properties at 1710-1730 East Pender Street from its current designation to Comprehensive Development, or CD-1. CD-1 is a special zoning category that tailors land-use rules to a specific site, allowing more flexibility than standard zones. Council has not yet made a decision – this item is up for approval at an upcoming meeting. The named councillors must review the proceedings to be eligible to vote.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1710-1730 East Pender Street)
(Councillor Maloney, Orr and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on rezoning 6486 Chester Street to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 6486 Chester Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. This means the land would follow a custom set of rules instead of the standard zoning, allowing a specific development plan. The decision is coming up at a council meeting. Three councillors—Bligh, Fry, and Mayor Sim—must review the proceedings before they can vote. If approved, this rezoning could change what can be built on that site.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing to have your say on the proposed rezoning of 6486 Chester Street.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (6486 Chester Street)
(Councillors Bligh, Fry and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on rezoning 1302-1318 East 12th Avenue to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone several properties at 1302-1318 East 12th Avenue to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone allows the city to approve a specific development plan tailored to that site, instead of following the standard zoning rules. Council has not yet made a final decision – this item is up for approval at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1302 - 1318 East 12th Avenue)
(Councillors Klassen, Maloney, Montague, Orr and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider rezoning for 888-896 Cambie St and 111-191 Smithe St
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver Council will consider a proposed rezoning for 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street. The rezoning would change the land-use designation from DD (Downtown) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development), increasing maximum FSR from 5.0 to 19.5 to allow a 29-storey mixed-use building with 165 rental units, 246 hotel rooms, and ground-floor commercial. The heritage facade of the Stanley Brock Building at 888 Cambie Street would be protected by a heritage designation by-law. A public hearing will be scheduled before any final decision due to the required heritage by-law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date for this rezoning application and be ready to submit your comments.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	CD-1 Rezoning: 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street
Referral Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed rezoning: Council to consider CD-1 at 888 West 8th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will consider rezoning 888 West 8th Avenue from C-3A to CD-1 to allow a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor retail. The proposal increases maximum floor space ratio from 3.0 to 8.1 and maximum building height from 9.2 m (30 ft) to 50.6 m (166 ft). The application is consistent with the Official Development Plan, so a public hearing is not required, but Council may still refer it to one.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the April 14, 2026 Council meeting; if no public hearing is called, the decision could be final at that meeting.

key_dates.label: Council meeting – 888 West 8th Avenue CD-1 rezoning decision

agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	CD-1 Rezoning: 888 West 8th Avenue
Referral Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to fast-track rental housing & small hotels in new mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council is set to consider a referral report that would explore creating a new mixed-use commercial district (C-2A). The proposed plan would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels in certain areas, making it easier and quicker for developers to build these projects. A final decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and possibly vote on the next steps in an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public hearing dates and submit your feedback to Vancouver City Council before any vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	Fast-Tracking Rental Housing and Small Hotels – New Mixed-Use Commercial District
Referral Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update Amenity Cost Charge bylaw under Bill 16
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will direct staff to consult on updated density bonus and inclusionary zoning rules (Bill 16 compliance) and a new Amenity Cost Charge (ACC) bylaw. Staff will bring Bill 16 bylaw amendments to Council on May 5, 2026, and the ACC bylaw on July 28, 2026. Density bonus rules must be updated by June 30, 2026 per provincial law. Consultation includes the public and development industry stakeholders (UDI, NAIOP, HAVN).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit comments or attend consultation sessions on the proposed Amenity Cost Charge bylaw and Bill 16 density bonus/inclusionary zoning updates.

key_dates.label: Bill 16 zoning bylaw amendments referred to Vancouver Council
Provincial deadline for density bonus rule updates
ACC bylaw proposed for Vancouver Council consideration

agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Up for approval: Burnaby council to consider rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw to rezone 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue from R3-3 (Residential) to R5-4 (Residential) district, enabling a 20-storey building with 172 rental units, 20% of residential floor area designated as below-market rental (BMR). This is still a proposal — council has not made a final decision. The public is not scheduled to speak at this session per the procedure bylaw.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will consider a proposed bylaw to rezone 310 East 14th Avenue from R3-3 to CD-1, allowing a 20-storey rental building with 138 units (20% below-market rental). The floor space ratio would increase from 2.70 to 6.53 and height from 23 m to 60 m. No decision has been made; no public hearing will be held.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the council vote on April 14, 2026 – approval could affect nearby property values and development plans.

key_dates.label: Council vote on rezoning – 310 East 14th Avenue

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 rezoning for 3803-3823 W 10th Ave & 2553 Highbury St, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposed rezoning from RR-3B to CD-1 at 3803-3823 W 10th Ave & 2553 Highbury St, Vancouver. The six-storey mixed-use building would contain 82 secured market rental units and a minimum 594.9 m² of commercial space at grade. A time-limited condition requires at least 20% of residential floor area to be below-market rental if a stage one building permit is not issued within two years of Council approval. Maximum FSR is 3.50, height 22 m, and at least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. No public speakers allowed; written comments accepted before the vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Submit written comments to Vancouver City Council before the April 14, 2026 vote.

key_dates.label: Council vote on CD-1 rezoning - Vancouver

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on CD-1 rezoning for 4088 Granville Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw to rezone 4088 Granville Street from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. If approved, the rezoning would increase maximum floor space ratio from 0.5 to 1.2 and building height from 11.5 m to 13.4 m, permitting three three-storey residential rental buildings containing 31 units. At least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms for family housing. The application is by Matthew Cheng Architect Inc. on behalf of Kirin Investment Ltd.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor the council vote on April 14, 2026 — approval could affect surrounding property values and development opportunities in the First Shaughnessy area.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 4088 Granville Street CD-1 rezoning

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 text amendment for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Ave, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a bylaw amendment to change CD-1 (839) zoning for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment would replace the existing requirement for for-profit affordable rental housing (30% of units for households with incomes below HILs at rents ≤30% of income) with 100% secured market rental housing. The floor space ratio (FSR) would increase from 3.49 to 3.5. The draft by-law requires at least 35% of units to have two or more bedrooms and a maximum building height of 22.3 m.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on miscellaneous housing rezoning condition amendments
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider amendments to 11 housing rezoning conditions (addresses include 1434-1456 West 8th Ave, 2090 West Broadway, etc.) to implement the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP). This two-year pilot (Feb 1, 2026 – Dec 15, 2027) allows eligible mid- and high-rise below-market rental projects to adjust affordability requirements—from 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents to rates not exceeding city-wide averages (up to 25% discount vs 2024 market rents). Eligibility: projects in community plan areas, fewer than 10 existing purpose-built rental units, under 8,000 sqm, no Stage 1 building permit (with exceptions for permits issued Dec 15, 2025 – Mar 20, 2026).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Developers with eligible BMR projects should review the RDRP criteria and consider applying before the pilot ends December 15, 2027.

key_dates.label: Council vote on housing rezoning amendments and RDRP
Rental Development Relief Program pilot ends

agenda_briefs.source_body:

(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider funding application for local development approvals program
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will consider endorsing a $200,000 funding application to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program (LGDAP) for the Vancouver Housing Service Redesign: Streamlined Processes and Reusable Blueprints project. If approved, the project would redesign end-to-end approvals for large-scale housing developments, creating reusable guides and templates. The City previously received $500,000 from LGDAP for a related project. This could reduce wait times for construction and development businesses.

key_dates.label: Vancouver City Council meeting to consider funding application

agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on 2026-2029 public plaza alcohol sites
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider proposed sites for the 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program. This program would allow people to drink alcohol in certain public plazas during designated times. Council is set to vote on which plazas will be included — no decision has been made yet. The proposed list of sites will be discussed at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation 2025 Annual Report
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a recommendation to accept the Vancouver Heritage Foundation's 2025 Annual Report and approve a $152,375 operating grant for 2026, the first year of a three-year agreement (2026–2028) with 2% inflationary increases in subsequent years. The recommendation (Recommendation B) requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as required under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy up for council consideration
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will consider adopting a citywide Coastal Flood Management Policy at its April 14, 2026 meeting. The policy clarifies the City's role in coastal flood management, emphasizes using land-use authority and redevelopment to incrementally improve flood resilience, and establishes that new City-built structural measures (dikes, tide gates) will be considered only for protecting City facilities and core services, subject to financial capacity. If passed, property owners, developers, and businesses in coastal flood-risk areas could face new requirements for flood protection and construction standards. This is still a proposal — no final decision has been made.

key_dates.label: Vancouver City Council meeting – Coastal Flood Management Policy vote

agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council set to vote on proposed 2025 property tax exemptions
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed 2025 property tax exemption bylaw. The proposal would provide tax relief to certain property categories, though the exact qualifying properties and exemption amounts are still under consideration. No public hearing or speaker session is planned, so owners should stay informed through council meeting minutes and updates. If passed, the exemptions would apply to the 2025 tax year, potentially lowering costs for eligible property owners and their tenants.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on 2025 property tax exemptions — the outcome could affect your property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax levy distribution changes under review
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a report on property tax levy distribution — a proposal that could change how property taxes are split between residential, commercial, and industrial properties. If approved, this would affect how much business owners pay in property taxes. Nothing has been decided yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Monitor council proceedings – a change in tax distribution could affect your property tax bill. Attend any public hearing if announced.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on proposed property tax levy for Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will vote on a budget resolution to adopt the 2026 General Purposes Tax Levy of $1,224,250,000. The levy is based on estimated gross expenditures of $2,392,516,008 and non-property tax revenues of $1,168,266,008. If adopted, the levy will set the amount of property tax owed by commercial and residential property owners for the year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision – the new tax levy will determine your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 4a. Budget Resolution
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 property tax levy distribution up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal for how the 2026 property tax levy will be split among different property classes, such as commercial, residential, and industrial. This decision affects how much tax businesses and homeowners will pay. The proposal is not yet law — council will consider it at an upcoming meeting. Business owners who own or lease commercial property in Burnaby should watch this decision closely, as a change in the distribution could raise or lower their property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council's vote on this proposal — the outcome could directly change your commercial property taxes.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026 up for Council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council is set to vote on a recommendation to approve 19 Spring 2026 Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 from the $244,000 2026 budget. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds approval per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. This is the first of two intakes; the second intake is in Fall 2026. If approved, the grants will fund specific local sport events.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026. If you missed this intake, the Fall 2026 intake will offer a second opportunity for grant applications.

key_dates.label: Council vote on Spring 2026 Community Sport Event Grants

agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 2026 Cultural Grants for operating and spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to approve 2026 Cultural Grants, including operating grants and funding for cultural spaces. The vote requires at least a two-thirds majority of all council members under a special rule in the Vancouver Charter (Section 206(1)). This is a proposed decision — the council has not yet made a final choice. If approved, the grants would support local arts and cultural organizations in Burnaby.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive: 2025 Vancouver Homeless Count report
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council is set to receive the 2025 Homeless Count report. On March 11, 2025, 2,715 people were identified as homeless (1,952 sheltered, 763 unsheltered), a 12% increase from 2023. 79% of respondents reported being homeless for over a year. No decision is being made at this time.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver council approved calling on senior governments for homeless supports
minutes_briefs.summary: Council received the 2025 Homeless Count report from Vancouver and voted unanimously to call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to increase income assistance, disability and rent supports, address intimate partner violence, re-commit to a homeless navigation centre, expand mental health and substance use treatment services across Metro Vancouver and BC, strengthen discharge planning from health care, corrections, and child welfare systems, and provide support to local governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
1. 2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
April 1, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Dominato
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to: 

a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;

b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre; 

c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community; 

d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and

e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering. 

amended 

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Maloney

THAT the words “and housing with supports” be added to the end of e.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11512)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11513) with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver” for information.

B. THAT Council call on the Provincial and Federal Governments to: 

a. Increase investments in income assistance, disability and rent supports, and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to homelessness;

b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre; 

c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and recovery services across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access close to home and community; 

d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being discharged into homelessness; and

e. Provide support to Local Governments for daytime sheltering and housing with supports.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver City Council approved $9.7M in cultural grants for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Council approved $9.7 million in cultural grants for 2026. This includes $2.8 million in operating grants to five major institutions, $709,760 in annual grants to 26 organizations (plus an extra $25,000 for the Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society), and $5.69 million in multi-year grants to 52 organizations over three years. Council also approved amended conditions for a $175,000 Making Space for Arts and Culture grant, an additional $75,000 for site assessments at the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society, and a one-time $728,000 grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House for hazardous materials removal and accessibility upgrades. Staff were authorized to finalize grant agreements.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
2. 2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
March 30, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Fry
SECONDED by Councillor Zhou

A. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding - Institutions grants totalling $2,828,160 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the five organizations listed in Section A of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those five organizations to $3,783,680.

B. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding – Annual grants totalling $709,760 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the 26 organizations listed in Section B of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those 26 organizations to $1,097,000.

C. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding – Multi-year grants of $1,897,550 each year for three years (2026, 2027, 2028) totalling $5,692,650 from the Annual Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the 52 cultural organizations listed in Section C of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which totals $1,168,850 for the same report. 

D. THAT Council approve amended conditions as further described on page 6 under the section titled “Recommendations D to F: Making Space for Arts and Culture Grant” of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the disbursement of the Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of $175,000 approved by Council on December 10, 2025, (Council Report RTS 18091) to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study. 

E. THAT Council approve a Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of an additional $75,000 to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support additional site assessment needs for the development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve. 

F. THAT, subject to the approval of E above, Council approve an increase to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan and the Multi-year Capital Project Budget for the Cultural Spaces program by $75,000, to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve. 

G. THAT Council approve a one-time grant of $728,000 ($428,000 to be funded from the 2026 Capital Budget for Joy Kogawa House Renewal, RTS 14879, and $300,000 to be funded from the 2026 REFM approved Capital budget) to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society to support hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades. 

H. THAT Council delegate its authority to execute grant agreements that include terms and conditions that are consistent with this report, that satisfy the City’s Director of Legal Services, and that disburse the grants described in this report to the satisfaction of the City’s General Manager of Arts, Culture, and Community Services (or their designate).

I. THAT pursuant to Section 206(1)(j) of the Vancouver Charter, Council deems any organization listed in Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” that is not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency to be contributing to the culture of Vancouver. 

amended

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Zhou
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT the words “and that an additional $25,000 be added to this grant stream from reserves and allocated to The Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society of Vancouver” be added to the end of B.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11514)
(Councillor Bligh absent for the vote)

The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY AND A to C, E and G BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11515).

FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED

A. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding - Institutions grants totalling $2,828,160 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the five organizations listed in Section A of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those five organizations to $3,783,680.

B. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding – Annual grants totalling $709,760 from the 2026 Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the 26 organizations listed in Section B of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which will bring the 2026 total operating grants for those 26 organizations to $1,097,000, and that an additional $25,000 be added to this grant stream from reserves and allocated to The Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society of Vancouver.

C. THAT Council approve Cultural Operating Funding – Multi-year grants of $1,897,550 each year for three years (2026, 2027, 2028) totalling $5,692,650 from the Annual Cultural Grants Operating Budget to the 52 cultural organizations listed in Section C of Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the amounts recommended for each organization in the “2026 Grant Recomm” column, which totals $1,168,850 for the same report. 

D. THAT Council approve amended conditions as further described in the section titled “Recommendations D to F” on page 6 of this Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the disbursement of the Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of $175,000 approved by Council on December 10, 2025, (Council Report RTS 18091) to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study. 

E. THAT Council approve a Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of an additional $75,000 to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support additional site assessment needs for the development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve. 

F. THAT, subject to the approval of E above, Council approve an increase to the 2023-2026 Capital Plan and the Multi-year Capital Project Budget for the Cultural Spaces program by $75,000, to be funded from the Coal Harbour Arts Complex Reserve. 

G. THAT Council approve a one-time grant of $728,000 ($428,000 to be funded from the 2026 Capital Budget for Joy Kogawa House Renewal, RTS 14879, and $300,000 to be funded from the 2026 REFM approved Capital budget) to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society to support hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades. 

H. THAT Council delegate its authority to execute grant agreements that include terms and conditions that are consistent with this report, that satisfy the City’s Director of Legal Services, and that disburse the grants described in this report to the satisfaction of the City’s General Manager of Arts, Culture, and Community Services (or their designate).

I. THAT pursuant to Section 206(1)(j) of the Vancouver Charter, Council deems any organization listed in Appendix A of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” that is not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency to be contributing to the culture of Vancouver.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $89,000 in community sport event grants for spring 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved 19 community sport event grants totalling $89,000. The grants come from the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program budget and are awarded to organizations listed in the report. Each recipient must agree to the program's terms and conditions, and no legal rights or obligations exist until a signed grant agreement is delivered.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
3. Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
March 10, 2026

A. THAT Council approve 19 new Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 as outlined in the body of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” to each organization listed in Table 1 of the same report, under the column entitled “Organization” and for the amount set out beside their name in the column entitled “Grant Recommended”; source of funding is the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget.

B. THAT the grants in A above be subject to each grant recipient agreeing to the terms and conditions of the community sport hosting grant program as generally outlined in this report and otherwise satisfactory to the Director, Tourism and Destination Events and the City Solicitor. 

C. THAT no legal rights or obligations are created by the approval of the A and B above unless and until the grant agreement letter is executed and delivered by the grant recipient. 

D. THAT pursuant to the Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter, the organizations listed in Table 1 of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” which are not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency are deemed by Council to be an organization contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or welfare of the City. 

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND A BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11523)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council adopted Coastal Flood Management Policy
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has adopted the Coastal Flood Management Policy, which outlines the city's role in planning and managing structural and non-structural measures to protect against coastal flooding and sea level rise. The policy was approved unanimously, but an amendment to create a task force for the False Creek floodplain was defeated.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Vancouver property owners and developers should review the new policy to understand how it may affect floodplain planning and development requirements.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
6. Coastal Flood Management Policy
March 17, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy,” which articulates the City’s role and supports planning, implementation, and management of structural and non-structural coastal flood management measures in Vancouver.

carried

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney
SECONDED by Councillor Orr

A. THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy.”

B. THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and inter-agency task force for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of:
1. identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise;
2. assessing whether a coordinated, area-wide structural flood management approach would be more effective and cost-efficient than fragmented site-by-site measures;
3. identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation in respect of Granville Island, the Department of National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place, Providence Health Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro, Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as staff may determine;
4. identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required to support any future funding strategy or senior-government engagement; and
5. reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council decisions on capital commitments.

C. THAT the report back in B above also assesses whether similar multi-jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek-specific approach is warranted.

LOST (Vote No. 11516)
(Councillors Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11517) with Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $152,375 grant to Vancouver Heritage Foundation for 2026 operations
minutes_briefs.summary: Council approved the 2025 Annual Report of the Vancouver Heritage Foundation and authorized payment of a $152,375 grant from the 2026 Operating Budget. This grant covers the first year of a three-year operating agreement (2026–2028). The decision was adopted on consent with one councillor absent.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7. Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
March 6, 2026

A. THAT Council approve the 2025 Annual Report of the Vancouver Heritage Foundation (VHF) as attached at Appendix A of the Report dated March 6, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025.”

B. THAT Council approve payment of a grant from the 2026 Operating Budget to the Vancouver Heritage Foundation in the amount of $152,375.00 to be used as operating funds for the first year of the three-year operating agreement (2026-2028).

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND B BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11527)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved amendments to designate Burnaby public plazas for alcohol consumption through 2029
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved amendments to the City Land Regulation By-law to designate specific public plazas where people can drink alcohol. The program, called the Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program, runs from 2026 to 2029 and allows liquor consumption in designated public spaces under City jurisdiction.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
8. Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
March 17, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Klassen
SECONDED by Councillor Fry

A. THAT Council approve, in principle, amendments to the City Land Regulation By-law No. 8735 to designate certain public places over which the City has jurisdiction as places where liquor may be consumed, to conclude in 2028.

B. THAT the Director of Legal Services be instructed to bring forward for enactment by Council a By-law to amend the City Land Regulation By-law to designate these places, generally as set out in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program.”

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11518)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved $200,000 grant application for housing service redesign
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to endorse a $200,000 funding application to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program. The grant would support the Vancouver Housing Service Redesign project, which aims to streamline development approval processes and create reusable blueprints. Council also approved related adjustments to the 2026 Operating Budget for the Development, Building & Licensing department, pending a contribution agreement. The decision was adopted on consent, with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
9. Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
March 17, 2026

A. THAT Council endorse a funding application in the amount of $200,000 submitted to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program for the project Vancouver Housing Service Redesign: Streamlined Processes and Reusable Blueprints. 

B. THAT subject to the execution of a contribution agreement, Council approve the related adjustments to the 2026 Operating Budget for the Development, Building & Licensing (DBL) department as described in the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program.”

ADOPTED ON CONSENT (Vote No. 11528)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby City Council approved amendments to housing rezoning conditions for multiple sites.
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved amendments to housing rezoning conditions for several developments, as detailed in Appendix A of the April 2, 2026 report. The changes will be integrated into the respective Housing Agreements, but only if each property owner agrees. Council also instructed staff to prepare the necessary by-laws to formally adopt the amendments. The resolution does not create any legal rights or obligations, and the City retains full discretion in exercising its authority.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own a development listed in Appendix A, review the amended housing rezoning conditions and contact the City to finalize your Housing Agreement.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
10. Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
April 2, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Zhou
SECONDED by Councillor Montague

A. THAT Council approve the amendments to the housing rezoning conditions generally as presented in Appendix A of the Report dated April 2, 2026, entitled “Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions,” and instruct the General Manager of Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and the Director of Legal Services to integrate the amended conditions into the respective Housing Agreements for the developments listed in Appendix A of the same report, subject to the agreement by the respective owner.

B. THAT subject to approval of A above, the Director of Legal Services be instructed to prepare the necessary Housing Agreement By-laws for enactment, subject to such terms and conditions as may be required at the discretion of the Director of Legal Services, and the General Manager of Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability.

C. THAT A and B above be adopted on the following conditions: 

i. THAT the passage of the above resolutions creates no legal rights for the applicant or any other person, or obligation on the part of the City; and any expenditure of funds or incurring of costs is at the risk of the person making the expenditure or incurring the cost; and 

ii. THAT the City and all its officials, including the Approving Officer, shall not in any way be limited or directed in the exercise of their authority or discretion, regardless of when they are called upon to exercise such authority or discretion.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11519)
(Councillor Orr abstained from the vote)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 text amendment for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved an amendment to the CD-1 bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment increases the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5 and changes the rental housing from affordable rental leased by a non-profit to secured market rental housing. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11. CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
March 10, 2026

MOVED by Councillor Klassen
SECONDED by Councillor Zhou

A. THAT the application by JTA Development Consultants, on behalf of Nicolaou Properties Ltd., the registered owners of the lands located at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue [PID 031-730-329; Lot A Block 16 District Lot 394 Group 1 New Westminster District Plan EPP119357] to amend CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District (839) By-law No. 13493 to increase the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5 and to change the secured rental housing from for-profit affordable rental housing to be leased by a non-profit corporation, with 30% of the dwelling units to be occupied by households with incomes below housing income limits (HILs) and each rented at a rate no higher than 30% of the aggregate household income, to secured market rental housing, be approved in principle;

FURTHER THAT the draft by-law to amend CD-1 (839) By-law No. 13493, prepared in accordance with Appendix A of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue,” be approved in principle;

FURTHER THAT the proposed form of development also be approved in principle, generally as prepared by Studio One Architecture Inc., received July 18, 2025, and amended plans received on October 29, 2025; 

AND FURTHER THAT the above approvals be subject to the Conditions of Approval contained in Appendix B of the above-noted report.

B. THAT subject to approval in principle of the rezoning and the Housing Agreement described in Part 2 of Appendix B of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue,” the Director of Legal Services be instructed to prepare the necessary Housing Agreement By-law for enactment prior to enactment of the amended CD-1 By-law, subject to such terms and conditions as may be required at the discretion of the Director of Legal Services and the General Manager of Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability.

C. THAT A and B above be adopted on the following conditions:
(i) THAT the passage of the above resolutions creates no legal rights for the applicant or any other person, or obligation on the part of the City and any expenditure of funds or incurring of costs is at the risk of the person making the expenditure or incurring the cost;
(ii) THAT any approval that may be granted shall not obligate the City to enact a by-law rezoning the property, and any costs incurred in fulfilling requirements imposed as a condition of rezoning are at the risk of the property owner; and
(iii) THAT the City and all its officials, including the Approving Officer, shall not in any way be limited or directed in the exercise of their authority or discretion, regardless of when they are called upon to exercise such authority or discretion.

CARRIED (Vote No. 11520)
(Councillor Orr opposed)
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 4088 Granville Street rental project
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, changing the land from FSD (First Shaughnessy) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) district. This allows three three-storey rental buildings with 31 units, increasing the maximum floor space ratio from 0.5 to 1.2 and maximum building height from 11.5 m to 13.4 m. The approval is subject to conditions including a housing agreement to be finalized before the new bylaw takes effect.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for mixed-use building at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning to allow a six-storey mixed-use building with ground-floor commercial space and 82 rental housing units at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The developer must include below-market rental units if a building permit is not issued within two years. Council also approved updates to the sign and noise bylaws for the site.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 20-storey rental building at 310 East 14th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning application for 310 East 14th Avenue, allowing a 20-storey residential rental building. The property was rezoned from R3-3 (Residential) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development), which increases the maximum floor space ratio from 2.70 to 6.53 and the maximum height from 23 m (75 ft) to 60 m (197 ft). At least 20% of the residential floor area must be rented at below-market rates. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a rezoning application for the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. The lands were rezoned from R3-3 (Residential) to R5-4 (Residential), allowing for higher-density residential development. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement to secure affordable housing. The decision was adopted on consent at the March 24, 2026 council meeting.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
15. Rezoning: 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue
March 24, 2026

A. THAT the application by Canderel Pacific Realties Inc., on behalf of:
• Sylton Holding & Management Ltd., the registered owner of 1305 West 13th Avenue [Lot 11 and 12 Block 412 District Lot 526 Plan 1276; PIDs 014-681-471 and 014-681-480]; and
• South Granville Holdings Ltd., the registered owner of 1325 West 13th Avenue [PID 014-681-498; Lot 13 Block 412 District Lot 526 Plan 1276];
to rezone the lands from R3-3 (Residential) District to R5-4 (Residential) District, be approved in principle;

FURTHER THAT the draft zoning amendment by-law, prepared in accordance with Appendix A of the Report dated March 24, 2026, entitled “Rezoning: 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue,” be approved in principle; 

AND FURTHER THAT the above approvals be subject to the Conditions of Approval contained in Appendix B of the above-noted report.

B. THAT subject to approval in principle of the rezoning and the Housing Agreement described in Part 2 of Appendix B of the Report dated March 24, 2026, entitled “Rezoning: 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue,” the Director of Legal Services be instructed to prepare the necessary Housing Agreement By-law for enactment prior to enactment of the zoning amendment By-law, subject to such terms and conditions as may be required at the discretion of the Director of Legal Services and the General Manager of Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability. 

C. THAT A to B above be adopted on the following conditions:
(i) THAT the passage of the above resolutions creates no legal rights for the applicant or any other person, or obligation on the part of the City and any expenditure of funds or incurring of costs is at the risk of the person making the expenditure or incurring the cost;
(ii) THAT any approval that may be granted shall not obligate the City to enact a by-law rezoning the property, and any costs incurred in fulfilling requirements imposed as a condition of rezoning are at the risk of the property owner; and
(iii) THAT the City and all its officials, including the Approving Officer, shall not in any way be limited or directed in the exercise of their authority or discretion, regardless of when they are called upon to exercise such authority or discretion.

ADOPTED ON CONSENT (Vote No. 11531)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby council approves consultation on Amenity Cost Charge By-law and zoning updates
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to direct staff to consult with the public and development industry stakeholders on the proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and related updates to the Zoning and Development By-law under Bill 16. The consultation is required before the bylaw can be adopted. Developers and builders should watch for upcoming consultation opportunities to provide input.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are a developer or builder in Burnaby, monitor the city's website for consultation details and consider providing your input on the proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
16. 2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By-law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
March 31, 2026

THAT Council, being aware of its obligation to provide at least one opportunity for consultation, direct staff to consult on the proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law and Bill 16 Updates to the Zoning and Development By-law with:

a. the public; and

b. the following persons, organizations, and authorities it considers will be affected: Development industry stakeholders, including Urban Development Institute (UDI), National Association of Industrial and Office Properties (NAIOP); and Homebuilders Association Vancouver (HAVN).

ADOPTED ON CONSENT (Vote No. 11533)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved eight by-laws including rezoning and housing agreements
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously enacted eight by-laws at a recent meeting. The by-laws rezone several properties to Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zones, which are special zoning rules for specific developments. Other by-laws amend housing agreements and authorize property assessments to cover costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project and the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project. All by-laws were passed with some councillors abstaining due to conflicts of interest.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
BY-LAWS

Councillor Klassen advised he had reviewed the proceedings related to By-law 1 and would therefore be voting on the enactment. 

Councillor Fry advised he had reviewed the proceedings related to By-law 2 and would therefore be voting on the enactment. 

MOVED by Councillor Dominato
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

THAT Council, except for those members ineligible to vote as noted below, enact the by-laws listed on the agenda for this meeting as numbers 1 to 8 inclusive, and authorize the Mayor and City Clerk to sign and seal the enacted by-laws.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY
(Councillor Bligh absent for the vote)

1. A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1302 - 1318 East 12th Avenue) (By-law No. 14663)
(Councillors Maloney, Montague, Orr and Mayor Sim ineligible to vote)

2. A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (6486 Chester Street) (By-law No. 14664)
(Councillor Bligh and Mayor Sim ineligible to vote)

3. A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1710-1730 East Pender Street) (By-law No. 14665)
(Councillors Maloney, Orr and Mayor Sim ineligible to vote)

4. A By-law to amend CD-1 (198) By-law No. 6254 (1700-1750 West 75th Avenue) (By-law No. 14666)
(Councillor Montague ineligible to vote.)

5. A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street) (By-law No. 14667)

6. A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue (By-law No. 14668)

7. A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project (By-law No. 14669)

8. A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project (By-law No. 14670)

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby council refers Cambie Street rezoning and heritage designation to public hearing
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to refer a rezoning application for 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street to a public hearing. The proposal would allow a 29-storey mixed-use building with 165 rental units, 246 hotel rooms, and ground-floor commercial space. Council also approved in principle the heritage designation of the Stanley Brock Building's Cambie Street facade. The public hearing will give residents and business owners a chance to comment before final approval.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council approved CD-1 rezoning for 16-storey hotel at 888 West 8th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue, changing the zoning from C-3A (Commercial) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development). This permits a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor retail, increasing the maximum floor space ratio from 3.0 to 8.1 and maximum height from 9.2 m to 50.6 m. Council also approved updates to the Sign By-law and Noise Control By-law for the new zone. The decision was unanimous.

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby council approved new C-2A mixed-use commercial district, referred to public hearing
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to approve a new mixed-use commercial zoning district called C-2A, designed to fast-track rental housing and small hotels. Council referred the new district and related bylaw amendments (including changes to zoning, parking, sign, noise, and subdivision rules) to a public hearing for final adoption. The decision also updates the Rental Housing Stock and Downtown Eastside/Oppenheimer Official Development Plans to include the C-2A district.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 57.5% residential tax share for 2026 property taxes
minutes_briefs.summary: Council voted to set the 2026 property tax distribution so that residential properties cover 57.5% of the total levy and non-residential properties cover 42.5%. The Director of Finance has been instructed to calculate the corresponding tax rates. This decision is final and will affect property tax bills for homeowners and businesses in Burnaby for the 2026 tax year.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
4b. 2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
April 7, 2026

THAT Council instruct the Director of Finance to calculate the 2026 general purpose tax rates for all property classes to achieve a tax share of approximately 57.5% residential and approximately 42.5% non-residential.

ADOPTED ON CONSENT (Vote No. 11525)
(Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver City Council adopted 2026 budget with $2.39B in expenditures and $1.22B property tax levy
minutes_briefs.summary: Council adopted the 2026 budget, approving total gross expenditures of $2,392,516,008. The budget includes a General Purposes Tax Levy of $1,224,250,000, funded through property taxes and other revenues totalling $1,168,266,008. The resolution was passed on consent, with Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.
